behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from the recommendation results regardless of their relevance score.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is that the filter doesn't apply (or applies after ranking, allowing high-score out-of-stock items through). This is testable in isolation by passing a product list with high-relevance out-of-stock items and verifying none appear in output. The 'regardless of relevance score' condition is a test-data concern, not a module-boundary concern. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed — fake product objects suffice. LLM-DEP: Deterministic filter. REJECTION-GATE: Would escalate to Integration only if the filter and ranker are separate modules that could be wired incorrectly, but the behavior's failure mode (an out-of-stock item appearing) is catchable by testing the filter function's output directly.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a mixed product list containing in-stock items with low relevance scores and out-of-stock items with artificially high relevance scores. Pass this list through the availability filter function. Assert that zero out-of-stock products appear in the output, regardless of their relevance score values.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is in the filter predicate itself — it either reads the wrong field from user settings or applies incorrect logic. A unit test can stub user settings (blocked categories = ['electronics', 'toys']) and pass a catalog containing items from those categories, verifying they are excluded. REAL-INFRA: No external dependencies needed. LLM-DEP: Deterministic. REJECTION-GATE: Would escalate to Integration only if settings are fetched at filter-call time from a separate module and the failure is in how settings are passed across that boundary. The behavior describes filter correctness, not settings-fetch correctness.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user profile object with two explicitly blocked categories and a product catalog with items spanning those blocked categories and several unblocked categories. Invoke the preference filter function. Assert that all products in blocked categories are absent from the output and all products in permitted categories are present.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products that match the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the ranking algorithm's weight calculation — it either ignores the browsing signal or applies incorrect weights. This is testable by providing a controlled product set (some matching recent browses, some not) and a stub browsing history, then verifying rank ordering. REAL-INFRA: No real I/O needed. LLM-DEP: Behavior 10 asserts the system is deterministic, indicating a rule-based or weight-based algorithm rather than a non-deterministic ML model. REJECTION-GATE: Would escalate to Agentic if the ranker were a neural network with non-deterministic scoring, but determinism (behavior 10) disqualifies that path.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a product set where group A products match the stub user's recent browsing history and group B products have no browsing overlap. Call the ranking algorithm with this product set and browsing history. Assert that all group A products appear before all group B products in the ranked output.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results, with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The stability failure only manifests when the ranking module and pagination module are wired together and called twice — e.g., if the ranking uses a non-stable sort or a HashMap whose iteration order changes between calls. A unit test of the pagination slice function alone with a pre-sorted list cannot catch instability introduced by the upstream ranker. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed; an in-process wired pipeline catches the failure. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Would lower to Unit if pagination were tested against a hardcoded pre-sorted list, but then we'd miss instability caused by the ranker re-sorting differently on successive calls.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the full recommendation pipeline (filter → rank → paginate) with a fixed user state and static catalog. Request page 2 (e.g., items 11–20) twice in succession without any intervening state changes. Assert that both responses return exactly the same product IDs in exactly the same order, and that page 2 contains the correct offset slice relative to the total ranked list.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement), avoiding a filter bubble.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The serendipity requirement is most likely implemented as a post-processing module (diversity injector) wired after the main ranker. The failure — all results coming from browsed categories — only appears when the serendipity module is missing from or bypassed in the pipeline, not when any single function is wrong in isolation. REAL-INFRA: No real I/O needed; an in-process pipeline test with controlled catalog data catches this. LLM-DEP: Behavior 10 asserts determinism. REJECTION-GATE: Would lower to Unit only if serendipity is a single function whose output property can be verified without wiring to the ranker, but a standalone diversity function test would not catch the case where the diversity injector is never called in the pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Build a user profile whose entire browsing history covers categories A and B only. Load a product catalog containing items exclusively from A and B, plus several items from category C (never browsed). Run the full recommendation pipeline. Assert that at least one product from category C appears in the recommendation output.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the cold-start branch of the recommendation function — it either returns empty or fails to fall back to the popularity signal. This branch is reachable by calling the recommendation function with an empty history object and stub popularity data. REAL-INFRA: No real I/O needed. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Would escalate to Integration if popularity data is fetched from a separate module at call time and the failure is in that fetch-and-fallback wiring. But the behavior tests what the algorithm does with empty history, not how popularity data is fetched — stubbing popularity data is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user object with empty purchase history and empty browsing history. Provide stub popularity-ranked product data. Call the recommendation algorithm. Assert that the returned list is non-empty, and that the order of returned products matches the descending popularity ranking of the stub data rather than any personalization signal.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure modes are field-mapping bugs — product_id and position swapped, timestamp not captured, wrong list position used. These only surface when the tracking module reads from the recommendation context and writes to the storage module. A pure unit test with a mock store only verifies call arguments, which passes even if the tracking module extracts the wrong field names. An in-process fake store lets us read back the written record and assert all three fields are correct. REAL-INFRA: An in-memory/fake store is sufficient — real DB writes are not needed to catch field-mapping bugs. LLM-DEP: No.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the click-tracking module with an in-memory event store. Simulate a recommendation response containing 5 products in a known order. Trigger a click event on the 3rd product. Read the written record from the in-memory store. Assert that the stored event contains the exact product_id of the 3rd item, position value 3, and a timestamp within a reasonable window of the simulated click time.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: Performance at the 100K catalog scale depends on real execution: database index traversal, network round-trips, serialization costs, and OS scheduling — none of which are reproduced by in-process stubs. An in-memory list of 100K fake objects measures algorithmic complexity but hides the I/O-bound bottlenecks that dominate in production. The 200ms budget is a wall-clock SLA for a real HTTP response, not a CPU cycle budget. MOCK-HIDE: Any mock of the data layer will mask the real latency source, making the test always pass. REJECTION-GATE: Would lower to Integration only if the catalog is held entirely in application memory and the bottleneck is algorithm complexity — but a catalog of 100K products almost certainly lives in a database, making real I/O the critical measurement path.
behaviors.test_description: Seed a real test database with 100,000 products spanning realistic category and attribute distributions. Send an HTTP recommendation request for a representative user to the running recommendation service. Measure wall-clock response time from request send to response received. Assert the elapsed time is under 200ms. Run the benchmark at least 5 times and assert the p95 latency is under 200ms.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure only appears across two sequential steps: (1) a purchase event updates shared state, and (2) the recommendation algorithm reads that updated state and applies deprioritization logic. A unit test of the ranker alone cannot catch a bug where the purchase event is written to the wrong key, or where the ranker reads the right key but applies the wrong weight. The failure is at the module boundary between purchase recording and recommendation generation. REAL-INFRA: An in-process fake store shared between both modules reproduces the state-propagation failure without needing a real database. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Would escalate to System only if the purchase event flows through a real message queue that an in-process fake cannot replicate.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the purchase-recording module and recommendation module against a shared in-memory store. Call the recommendation pipeline for a user; record the rank of target product P. Record a purchase event for product P. Call the recommendation pipeline again for the same user state. Assert that product P's rank in the second response is strictly lower than its rank in the first response (or that it is absent from the top-N results if the deprioritization is strong enough).

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Non-determinism in a pipeline emerges from sources invisible in a single function call: HashMap iteration order, timestamp-based seeding, race conditions in shared caches, or the serendipity module using an unseeded RNG. These only surface when the full pipeline is called twice independently. A unit test of a single pure function cannot catch non-determinism introduced at module boundaries (e.g., the serendipity injector using a different random seed per request). REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed — an in-process pipeline with a fixed-state fake store is sufficient to observe whether two calls return the same output. LLM-DEP: The behavior explicitly asserts determinism; if an LLM were involved, determinism would require temperature=0 and identical seeds, but behavior 10 disqualifies a non-deterministic model as the ranker. REJECTION-GATE: Would lower to Unit only if the entire pipeline is a single pure function; 'two separate requests' implies stateful pipeline execution across multiple modules.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the full recommendation pipeline with a fixed user profile and a static in-memory catalog. Call the pipeline twice in sequence with no state changes between calls. Capture the full ranked product-ID list from each call. Assert that the two lists are identical in both content and order, including verifying that any serendipitous items (from behavior 5) appear at the same positions.
